The  present progressive of go (be going to ...) is often used as an auxiliary verb I'm  seeing Jack tomorrow. What are you doing this evening?

will + infinitive. For details, >231.

In many subordinate clauses we  refer to the future with present tenses instead of subordinate         clauses I knew she would arrive before long.

Something was going to happen that was to change the world.

past form of one of the future structures. For details, ►    43.

To  say that something was still in the future at a certain past time, we  can use a 'future     in  the   past' The President is to visit Beijing. You're not to tell anybody.

to give instructions. For details, ►    42.

Be to + infinitive is used to talk about plans, arrangements and schedules, and The plane's about to take off.

Be about to + infinitive (►  353) suggests that a future event is very close.

grammar •  35  going to For going to ... compared with the present progressive, ►    36.2.   For a comparison with will, ►    39.

For was going to, has been going to, etc, ►    43.

Nobody's gonna talk to me like that.

shown in writing as gonna, especially in American English.

In informal speech, going to is often pronounced /gənə/.  This is sometimes 5 gonna I'm not going to sit up all night listening to your problems!

It is also used in emphatic refusals.

You're not going to play football in my garden.

She's going to take that medicine whether she likes it or not!

You're going to finish that soup if you sit there all afternoon!

Going to ... can be used to insist that people do things or do not do things.

4 commands        and   refusals Look at the sky. It's going to rain. Look out! We're going to crash!

Sandra's going to have another baby in June.
